text: The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions, which are visible as fibrous tissue bands resulting from a prior hysterectomy.
The surgeon observes a loop of small bowel adhered to the incision site from the previous hysterectomy, with the omentum positioned downward.
The surgeon's left hand is visible near a stitch that closes a small serosal tear, which was edited out for speed.

The surgeon observes radiation damage causing fusion of the medial aspect of the mesentery, with the lateral aspects also fused. The right side is visibly fused to the pelvic sidewall, demonstrating classic radiation-induced changes.
The surgeon identifies the intraperitoneal rectum, which is fused to the right side, and begins medial mobilization to separate it from the surrounding fused tissue.

The surgeon encounters difficulty elevating the mesentery due to the sigmoid colon being adhered to the left pelvic sidewall. The surgeon prepares to address this adhesion to facilitate proper elevation and access beneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery and the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
The surgeon performs a medial mobilization of the colon without mobilizing the splenic flexure, achieving a tension-free anastomosis in the pelvis.
The surgeon completes the protecting ileostomy and proceeds to dissect down the left lateral side of the abdominal cavity, without the use of ureteral catheters.
The surgeon carefully checks the ureter, using ICG fluorescence to visualize the structure, which appears green under the imaging, ensuring its safety before continuing with the procedure.
The surgeon examines the left pelvic sidewall, noting the presence of dense adhesions that resemble radiation-induced changes rather than natural adhesions.

The surgeon observes the bladder flap adherent to the anterior surface of the intraperitoneal rectum, indicating complete fusion anteriorly. The surgeon continues to work down the left side to aid in mobilizing the left, right, and posterior sides. A quick check of the ureter is performed.

The surgeon frees up surrounding tissue to improve retraction of the rectum, addressing minor oozing by packing with a sponge. The sigmoid colon appears sufficiently mobilized, allowing the surgeon to begin the medial dissection.
The surgeon enters the posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, carefully navigating the hostile pelvic environment to identify the safe posterior plane while avoiding the presacral veins.

The surgeon has exposed the right side of the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, revealing the anatomical structures on both the left and right sides. The dissection progresses efficiently, with visible tissue separation occurring before the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is addressed.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, focusing on improving the mobility of the rectum. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is identified and cleaned for better visualization.
The surgeon encounters significant fibrosis in the area, suggesting previous radiation exposure in the lower abdomen. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is then isolated and prepared for ligation.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field, identifying the sponge previously placed for hemostasis. A careful examination reveals the ureter, which is now clearly visible, ensuring it is not mistaken for the gonadal vessels.
The surgeon briefly demonstrates the mobilization of the colon, noting that it was not necessary to navigate around the splenic flexure during this part of the procedure.
The surgeon attempts to perform the total mesorectal excision (TME) on the left anterior side, encountering difficulty in visualizing the distinct tissue plane.
The surgeon observes the mesorectal fat adherent to the bladder, noting the absence of the typical white crackly plane that is usually visible during dissection.

The surgical field appears oozy and extremely edematous, likely due to previous radiation treatment. The surgeon anticipates encountering scar tissue tethering on the back of the vagina, visible on MRI imaging, as they proceed deeper anteriorly.

The surgeon progresses deeper into the surgical field, improving visibility of the proper dissection plane. The suction device effectively clears the view, revealing an area of adherence as the surgeon moves anteriorly.

The surgeon switches to a vessel sealer to achieve better hemostasis and divide more tissue in a single movement, as the surgical field becomes deeper and oozing obscures the visibility of tissue planes.

The surgeon continues dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, taking broad bites with the instrument. The technique is less meticulous than scissor dissection, but careful management of thermal spread maintains effectiveness.

The surgeon identifies the stricture at approximately seven centimeters and works to reach a depth of four to five centimeters, just above the levator floor. The adherence to the back of the vagina is separated, allowing the surgeon to resume scissor dissection.

The surgeon struggles to maintain a clear view and handle the tissues while dissecting lower into the mesorectal area. Upon reaching approximately three centimeters below the stricture, normal mucosa is visible with some radiation changes. A tumor-specific mesorectal excision is performed just above the levator floor.

The surgeon completes the anterior dissection, exposing the anterior rectal wall approximately three centimeters below the stricture. Scissors are used for precise dissection, and the mesorectal tissue is excised around the rectum, revealing the posterior part.

The surgeon completes the circumferential division of the mesentery using four trocars placed in their normal positions. The mesentery appears damaged from manipulation, and a separate stoma aperture is visible in the right upper quadrant due to the patient's high BMI and short stature.
The surgeon palpates from below to ensure proper positioning in the surgical space. Hemostasis is achieved, and excess blobs of fat are cleaned up from the surgical field.
The surgeon removes a section of the mesorectum to achieve a more complete total mesorectal excision (TME), resulting in a complete pathologic response.

The surgeon undocks the robot and places the anvil, extracting the specimen through the ileostomy aperture. An anastomosis is created, followed by the formation of a diverting loop ileostomy. The surgical field appears hemostatic, with no drain left in place.
